profile.occupation: elementary school teacher

patient.chief_complaint: I’ve been getting more and more short of breath, even doing simple stuff like walking to the mailbox.

volunteer: I’ve been getting more and more short of breath, even doing simple stuff like walking to the mailbox.
I get tired really easily lately.
Sometimes I feel my heart pounding when I climb stairs.
I’ve had a dry cough that won’t quite go away.
if_asked: It’s worse when I try to move fast or go uphill—then I have to stop and catch my breath.
I’ve had a few moments where I felt lightheaded, like I might faint, especially when I exert myself.
My ankles have been a bit puffy by the end of the day.
I sleep propped up on a couple pillows because it feels easier to breathe.
Sometimes my lips or fingertips look a little bluish when I’m really winded.
I’ve noticed a tight feeling in my chest when I’m pushing myself, not exactly sharp pain.
hidden: My breathing seems to get noticeably worse after I get a cold, and it takes me a long time to bounce back.
I’ve started avoiding showers with hot steam because it makes me feel more short of breath.
I’ve lost a little weight without trying because eating big meals makes me feel winded.
I get a weird crackly feeling in my chest when I take a deep breath, but I thought it was just ‘congestion.’
I’ve had a couple of times where I woke up suddenly at night feeling like I couldn’t get enough air, then it settled after sitting up.
resistant: I’ve coughed up a small amount of blood a couple times—just streaks—but I didn’t want to bring it up because it scared me.
I’ve been using a friend’s ‘water pill’ a few times because the swelling bothered me, and I didn’t want to admit that.
misleading: I thought it might be asthma because I sometimes wheeze a little when I’m short of breath.
I figured it was anxiety because I get panicky when I can’t catch my breath.
I wondered if it was acid reflux because I sometimes have a sour taste and cough at night.
I thought maybe it was just being out of shape since I’ve been less active.
noise: My knees have been aching on and off, especially in the morning.
I’ve had some seasonal allergies with a runny nose.
I get occasional headaches when I’m stressed at work.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician specifically asks about coughing up blood or anything unusual in the mucus.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about nighttime breathing, sleeping position, and waking up short of breath.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about recent infections and whether symptoms flare after illnesses.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about all medications, including borrowed pills, supplements, or over-the-counter remedies.

general_concerns: I read that pulmonary hypertension medicines open up the blood vessels—so they should automatically make me breathe better, and if they don’t work it means the diagnosis is wrong.
Because my oxygen is low, I think this is basically asthma or anxiety attacks and I should be on inhalers instead of all these heart-lung tests.
Since I’m already on propranolol, I assumed it protects my lungs and prevents the blood pressure in my lungs from getting worse.
If my CT scan didn’t show a big blood clot, then my shortness of breath can’t be from the blood vessels—so it must be deconditioning.
If I push myself to exercise harder, I can ‘train’ my lungs to fix the problem and I don’t need to limit activity.
I thought home oxygen is addictive and will make my lungs get lazy if I use it too much.
economic_concerns: I’m worried the specialized tests and frequent follow-ups will be too expensive, so I’d rather wait until symptoms are severe before doing more workup.
If you recommend referral to a pulmonary hypertension center or transplant evaluation, I assume insurance won’t cover it and I’ll be stuck with huge bills.
I’m afraid I’ll have to stop working or take unpaid leave for appointments and I can’t afford to lose income.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start by volunteering: worsening shortness of breath with simple activity (mailbox), easy fatigue, occasional pounding heartbeat on stairs, and a persistent dry cough. If asked for details, add: worse with hills/fast pace, needing to stop; lightheaded episodes with exertion; ankle puffiness by evening; sleeping on 2 pillows; occasional bluish lips/fingertips when very winded; chest tightness with exertion (not sharp pain). Only reveal later or if specifically prompted: symptoms worsen after colds and recover slowly; hot steam/showers make breathing worse; mild unintentional weight loss because big meals make you winded; “crackly” feeling on deep breaths; waking at night gasping that improves sitting up. Resist disclosing unless directly asked in a nonjudgmental way: a few streaks of blood in sputum and using a friend’s water pill for swelling. Voice misconceptions: maybe asthma, anxiety, reflux, or being out of shape. Emphasize cost/work concerns and preference to delay testing. If clinician shows empathy, you calm slightly, thank them, and become more willing to share resisted details.

medications: Propranolol 20 mg PO twice daily
Sumatriptan 50 mg PO as needed for migraine (max 200 mg/day)
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/inhalation: 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze
confounders: Mild intermittent asthma causing episodic dyspnea/wheeze that can mask progressive pulmonary vascular disease
Propranolol use (can worsen bronchospasm and blunt tachycardic response; may confound dyspnea assessment)
Recent long-haul air travel 3 weeks ago (raises concern for pulmonary embolism despite normal D-dimer)
Active cigarette smoking (6 pack-years) contributing to exertional dyspnea and reduced DLCO on pulmonary function testing

acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (idiopathic/heritable) with suspicion for PVOD
Pulmonary hypertension due to pulmonary capillary hemangiomatosis (PCH spectrum disorder)
required_evidence: Clinical pulmonary hypertension with progressive exertional dyspnea and hypoxemia
High-resolution CT chest suggestive of PVOD (centrilobular ground-glass opacities, smooth interlobular septal thickening, mediastinal lymphadenopathy)
Markedly reduced DLCO out of proportion to spirometry
Right heart catheterization confirming precapillary pulmonary hypertension (elevated mPAP and PVR with normal/low PAWP)
Development or high risk of pulmonary edema with pulmonary vasodilator therapy (supports PVOD phenotype)
Exclusion of chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (e.g., V/Q scan without mismatched perfusion defects)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal; may be elevated (secondary erythrocytosis) if chronic hypoxemia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: Often normal; may be elevated if chronic hypoxemia
expected_lab_results.BMP - Sodium: Usually normal (may be low if advanced right heart failure with congestion/diuretics)
expected_lab_results.BMP - Potassium: Usually normal (monitor if diuretics are initiated)
expected_lab_results.BMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Usually normal; may be mildly elevated if chronic CO2 retention is present (not typical for isolated PVOD)

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (titrate to euvolemia)
Oxygen to maintain SpO2 >= 92% (route: nasal cannula; continuous or exertional as needed)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to a pulmonary hypertension specialty center
Early lung transplant evaluation (definitive therapy for PVOD)

Avoid/very cautious use of pulmonary vasodilators (e.g., PDE5 inhibitors, endothelin receptor antagonists, prostacyclins) due to risk of precipitating pulmonary edema; if considered, initiate only in expert center with close monitoring
Vaccinations: influenza and pneumococcal as indicated
Activity pacing and supervised pulmonary rehabilitation as tolerated; avoid high altitude and unpressurized flights if hypoxemic
Assess for and treat volume overload; daily weights and low-sodium diet if congested
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks with pulmonary hypertension specialist (earlier/urgent if worsening hypoxemia, syncope, edema, or signs of right heart failure)

must_include: PVOD is a rare cause of pulmonary hypertension due to scarring/blockage of small pulmonary veins
It can mimic pulmonary arterial hypertension but behaves differently
Why specific tests (CT features, DLCO, right heart catheterization, V/Q scan) are used to confirm and rule out other causes
Prognosis and the importance of specialty care and transplant evaluation
Supportive therapy focus: oxygen for hypoxemia and diuretics for fluid overload
Lung transplantation is the definitive treatment
Pulmonary vasodilators can cause dangerous pulmonary edema in PVOD and should not be started outside an expert center
When to seek urgent care (worsening shortness of breath, new/worsening leg swelling, chest pain, syncope, pink frothy sputum)
Medication review including current propranolol use and monitoring for low blood pressure/bradycardia if diuretics or other agents are added
Allergy to penicillin (urticarial rash) should be communicated to all clinicians/pharmacies
Shared decision-making about transplant evaluation and goals of care
Avoid high altitude and situations that worsen oxygen levels
Low-sodium diet and daily weights if fluid retention is present
Smoking cessation and avoidance of secondhand smoke
Keep vaccinations up to date and minimize respiratory infection risk

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, risk factors, functional status, red flags, and current meds/allergies to frame suspected PVOD vs other pulmonary hypertension etiologies and assess stability.

Order initial evaluation to support pulmonary hypertension workup and PVOD suspicion (e.g., BNP/NT-proBNP, CBC, CMP, LFTs, TSH, HIV, ANA/ENA as indicated) and assess safety for diuretics/other therapies.

Order pulmonary diagnostics relevant to PVOD confirmation and exclusion of alternatives: PFTs with DLCO, high-resolution CT chest, V/Q scan, echocardiogram (if not already), and plan for right heart catheterization at an expert center.

Review results to support PVOD pattern (markedly low DLCO, CT triad such as septal thickening/centrilobular ground-glass opacities/mediastinal lymphadenopathy) and to rule out CTEPH (V/Q) and left-heart disease (echo/RHC).
State working diagnosis (suspected PVOD) with differential and rationale; emphasize need for specialty pulmonary hypertension center confirmation.
Explain diagnosis milestone: PVOD pathophysiology, mimicry of PAH, why CT/DLCO/RHC/VQ are used, prognosis, and need for specialty care/transplant evaluation.
Perform critical allergy check prior to any prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy details and document/communicate.
Perform critical drug interaction check prior to prescribing; include propranolol and any planned diuretic/oxygen adjuncts or other supportive meds.

Initiate supportive therapy if indicated and safe (e.g., oxygen for hypoxemia; cautious diuretics for congestion) while avoiding empiric pulmonary vasodilators outside expert center due to pulmonary edema risk in PVOD.

Explain treatment milestone: supportive focus, transplant as definitive therapy, vasodilator pulmonary edema risk, and urgent-care triggers (worsening dyspnea, edema, chest pain, syncope, pink frothy sputum).

Address concerns milestone: medication review (propranolol, hypotension/bradycardia monitoring with diuretics/other agents), allergy communication, shared decision-making about transplant evaluation/goals of care.
Lifestyle milestone: avoid high altitude, low-sodium/daily weights if fluid retention, smoking cessation/avoid secondhand smoke, vaccinations and infection-risk reduction.

Arrange urgent referral to pulmonary hypertension specialty center and transplant evaluation; set close follow-up and monitoring plan (symptoms, weights, oxygen saturation, electrolytes/renal function if diuretics).
Close encounter with clear safety netting and follow-up plan.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed if the clinician asks about recent travel, outdoor exposure at dusk/night, or mosquito bites.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed only if the clinician asks gently about bladder control/accidents and normalizes that it can happen with severe illness.

general_concerns: Thinks the illness can spread by touching or sharing food with family members (like a cold).
Believes mosquito bites only matter at night and that daytime bites are harmless.
Thinks herbal balms and ‘cooling’ drinks can cure brain infection and may replace hospital treatment.
Worries that a vaccine given now could worsen the illness because he is already sick.
Believes seizures mean he is ‘possessed’ or has done something wrong, and feels ashamed.
economic_concerns: Family worries hospital tests and medicines will be unaffordable and asks to avoid ‘unnecessary’ scans or labs.
Caregivers fear losing daily wages if they stay at the hospital and ask for the fastest discharge possible.
Concerned that follow-up visits and rehabilitation (if needed) will cost too much and require travel they can’t afford.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Act anxious and a bit vague; you get confused and drift off mid-sentence. Start by saying you’ve had a very high fever for 2 days, a severe headache that won’t go away, and now you’re getting confused. Volunteer nausea/vomiting (twice) and extreme tiredness/sleepiness.

Only if asked, mention: stiff neck and pain looking down, bright lights hurt your eyes, you had a 1-minute shaking/jerking episode and couldn’t respond, you feel wobbly walking, and your family says you’re irritable/not yourself. If asked about exposures, say you had lots of mosquito bites 1–2 weeks ago near rural rice fields and pigs, and you didn’t get travel vaccines.

Do not offer unless directly asked: slurred speech, tremor/jerky movements, trouble peeing, sudden racing heart/sweats.

Resist sharing bedwetting, alcohol at a party, and using a friend’s vape—deny or change topic unless specifically asked privately.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe it’s a bad migraine/flu/COVID/food poisoning,” and fear “brain-eating amoeba.” If the clinician is empathetic, you calm slightly and answer more clearly. Caregivers push to avoid costly tests and want fast discharge.

medications: BiPAP nocturnal IPAP 16 cmH2O / EPAP 6 cmH2O nightly
Hydrocortisone 10 mg PO AM and 5 mg PO PM
Desmopressin 0.1 mg PO nightly
Oxybutynin 5 mg PO twice daily
Levetiracetam 500 mg PO twice daily
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever
Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours (empiric pending PCR results)
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV every 12 hours (empiric meningitis coverage)
comorbidities: Rapid-onset childhood obesity with hypothalamic dysfunction, hypoventilation, and autonomic dysregulation syndrome (ROHHAD)
Central hypoventilation with nocturnal ventilatory support
Possible secondary adrenal insufficiency due to hypothalamic dysfunction
confounders: Baseline hypoventilation from ROHHAD causing hypercapnia and somnolence that can mimic or worsen encephalopathy
Autonomic dysregulation (labile hypertension/tachycardia) complicating assessment of sepsis vs intracranial pathology
Chronic obesity and obstructive sleep-disordered breathing increasing risk of hypoxemia during acute illness and sedation for imaging/LP
Hyponatremia (possible SIADH from CNS infection vs hypothalamic dysfunction) contributing to altered mental status
Empiric acyclovir/ceftriaxone started before confirmatory testing, potentially obscuring early bacterial/viral differentiation by clinical course

acceptable_alternatives: Viral encephalitis (undifferentiated) pending confirmatory testing
Acute meningoencephalitis
required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with altered mental status and/or seizures consistent with encephalitis
Epidemiologic risk (travel/residence in JE-endemic area, mosquito exposure, rural/agricultural exposure, lack of vaccination, seasonal risk)
Neuroimaging/CSF consistent with viral encephalitis (when obtained): CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein, normal glucose; MRI thalamic/basal ganglia involvement can support
Confirmatory testing planned/obtained: JE virus IgM in CSF or serum (MAC-ELISA) and/or PCR early in illness (recognizing limited sensitivity)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Lymphocytes: Normal to mildly increased; relative lymphocytosis may be seen
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal (thrombocytopenia is not typical; if low, consider alternative diagnoses such as dengue)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: May be low (mild hyponatremia possible due to SIADH in CNS infection)

medications: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h
Levetiracetam 1-2 g IV/PO load then 500-1500 mg PO/IV BID (if seizures or high seizure risk)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR q6h PRN fever/pain
0.9% Sodium chloride IV titrate to euvolemia (avoid hypotonic fluids; adjust if hyponatremia/SIADH suspected)
non_pharmacological: Admit to hospital; ICU-level monitoring if decreased consciousness, seizures, or respiratory compromise
Airway/breathing/circulation stabilization; aspiration precautions; head-of-bed elevation
Seizure precautions and continuous neurologic monitoring
Evaluate and manage intracranial pressure if suspected (frequent neuro checks; consider neurocritical care measures per protocol)

Diagnostic workup while treating empirically: lumbar puncture for CSF studies (cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain/culture, HSV PCR, JE IgM), blood cultures if febrile, MRI brain and/or CT as indicated
Consult infectious diseases and neurology
Public health notification per local requirements; counsel on mosquito avoidance for contacts/travelers
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after stabilization and initiation of empiric therapy; daily reassessment with de-escalation when alternative causes excluded and JE testing results return (typically within 24-72 hours depending on lab).

must_include: This is encephalitis (brain inflammation) likely from a mosquito-borne virus; Japanese encephalitis is a leading consideration based on exposure risk
Diagnosis is clinical initially; confirmatory testing (CSF/serology) is being sent and may take time
Serious illness warning: risk of seizures, breathing problems, brain swelling, and long-term neurologic effects
No proven antiviral cure for Japanese encephalitis; treatment is supportive and focused on preventing complications
Empiric acyclovir is started now to cover HSV encephalitis until tests exclude it because delay can be harmful
What monitoring/treatments will occur: seizure control, fever control, fluids/electrolytes, ICU monitoring if needed
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and factors that worsen risk (decreased consciousness, seizures, need for ICU support)
Clarify transmission: not spread person-to-person in typical settings; mosquito-borne; standard precautions in hospital
Review medication safety: ask about kidney disease/dehydration (acyclovir), prior seizure meds, and confirm amoxicillin allergy details
Mosquito bite prevention (repellent, long sleeves, bed nets, avoiding dusk/dawn exposure)
Vaccination counseling for future travel/residence in endemic areas (JE vaccine) when clinically appropriate
After recovery: need for neurorehabilitation and follow-up for cognitive/neurologic sequelae

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: febrile illness with neurologic symptoms, exposure/travel to endemic areas, mosquito bites, vaccination status, timeline, and red flags (seizures, altered mental status, focal deficits).

Assess comorbidities and medication risks relevant to empiric therapy and supportive care: kidney disease/dehydration (acyclovir nephrotoxicity risk), immunosuppression, pregnancy, and baseline neurologic disease/antiepileptics.

Order initial encephalitis workup: CBC, CMP (renal function/electrolytes), glucose, LFTs, blood cultures as indicated; and diagnostic testing planning for CSF studies (cell count, protein/glucose, Gram stain/culture, HSV PCR, JE IgM/serology) plus other region-appropriate tests.

Order/arrange neurodiagnostics as part of encephalitis evaluation (e.g., brain MRI/CT as appropriate before LP if indicated, EEG if seizures/AMS) and document rationale for urgency/ICU monitoring triggers.
Review available results (noting that JE confirmatory tests may be pending) to support clinical diagnosis and to ensure safe dosing (especially creatinine for acyclovir).
State working diagnosis: encephalitis with Japanese encephalitis as leading consideration given exposure risk; maintain differential including HSV encephalitis requiring immediate empiric coverage.

Required safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies and reaction details (explicitly confirm amoxicillin allergy details) to avoid inappropriate antibiotic choices and to document allergy reconciliation.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., nephrotoxic agents with acyclovir; antiepileptic interactions if adding seizure meds).
After completing required safety checks, initiate empiric acyclovir promptly for possible HSV encephalitis while confirmatory testing is pending; include renal-dose consideration and hydration plan.

Order supportive treatments as needed: antipyretics, IV fluids/electrolyte correction, seizure rescue/maintenance meds if indicated, and ICU-level monitoring triggers (airway protection, refractory seizures, rising ICP concerns).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, testing timeline, seriousness/complications; explain treatment/supportive care and why acyclovir is started; address prognosis/transmission/med safety; provide mosquito prevention and future vaccine counseling.
Plan follow-up and recovery needs: neurology/infectious disease follow-up, rehabilitation assessment, and monitoring for cognitive/neurologic sequelae after acute illness.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, initial management, and patient/family understanding are addressed.

Order initial encephalitis workup: CBC, CMP (renal function/electrolytes), glucose, LFTs, blood cultures as indicated; and diagnostic testing planning for CSF studies (cell count, protein/glucose, Gram stain/culture, HSV PCR, JE IgM/serology) plus other region-appropriate tests.

Order/arrange neurodiagnostics as part of encephalitis evaluation (e.g., brain MRI/CT as appropriate before LP if indicated, EEG if seizures/AMS) and document rationale for urgency/ICU monitoring triggers.
Review available results (noting that JE confirmatory tests may be pending) to support clinical diagnosis and to ensure safe dosing (especially creatinine for acyclovir).
State working diagnosis: encephalitis with Japanese encephalitis as leading consideration given exposure risk; maintain differential including HSV encephalitis requiring immediate empiric coverage.

Required safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies and reaction details (explicitly confirm amoxicillin allergy details) to avoid inappropriate antibiotic choices and to document allergy reconciliation.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions (e.g., nephrotoxic agents with acyclovir; antiepileptic interactions if adding seizure meds).
After completing required safety checks, initiate empiric acyclovir promptly for possible HSV encephalitis while confirmatory testing is pending; include renal-dose consideration and hydration plan.

Order supportive treatments as needed: antipyretics, IV fluids/electrolyte correction, seizure rescue/maintenance meds if indicated, and ICU-level monitoring triggers (airway protection, refractory seizures, rising ICP concerns).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, testing timeline, seriousness/complications; explain treatment/supportive care and why acyclovir is started; address prognosis/transmission/med safety; provide mosquito prevention and future vaccine counseling.
Plan follow-up and recovery needs: neurology/infectious disease follow-up, rehabilitation assessment, and monitoring for cognitive/neurologic sequelae after acute illness.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, initial management, and patient/family understanding are addressed.

Agent states that diagnosis is initially clinical and that confirmatory testing (CSF and serology/JE IgM) is being sent and may take time to return.
Agent communicates high-risk warning signs and potential complications: seizures, respiratory failure/need for ventilation, cerebral edema/raised ICP, and long-term neurologic sequelae.
Agent initiates empiric acyclovir promptly for suspected encephalitis to cover HSV until excluded, and states the rationale that delay in HSV treatment can be harmful.
Agent clearly states there is no proven antiviral cure for Japanese encephalitis and that management is supportive and complication-focused.
Agent describes what monitoring/treatments will occur: seizure control, fever control, fluids/electrolytes, and ICU monitoring if needed.
Before any prescribing, agent performs critical safety checks: allergy check and contraindication check; the allergy check includes confirming details of the reported amoxicillin allergy.
Contraindication/safety screening includes asking about kidney disease and dehydration risk prior to acyclovir and acknowledges need for renal dosing and adequate hydration.
If drug interaction checking is performed, agent considers nephrotoxic co-medications and interactions with antiepileptics/sedatives used for seizure control.
Agent addresses prognosis uncertainty and identifies factors associated with worse outcomes (decreased consciousness, seizures, need for ICU/ventilatory support).
Agent clarifies transmission: Japanese encephalitis is mosquito-borne and not typically spread person-to-person; standard hospital precautions are appropriate.
Agent provides mosquito bite prevention counseling and mentions JE vaccination for future travel/residence in endemic areas when clinically appropriate.
Agent includes recovery planning: neurorehabilitation and follow-up for cognitive/neurologic sequelae.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 14-year-old 8th-grade girl. Act anxious and vague; you’re worried something serious is happening. Start with: “I keep having weird spells where my lips twitch and I blank out.” If asked to describe them, volunteer: brief staring spells where you can’t respond, lip/chewing-like jerks, sometimes you lose speech mid-sentence; afterward you’re briefly confused then normal. Don’t initially mention frequency, triggers, memory gaps, dropping things, shallow breathing/sighing, near-driving episode, hiding it from school, or energy drinks—only share these if specifically asked about timing, triggers, safety, school impact, or substances. If asked, say it happens many times daily, worse with stress/tiredness, flickering lights or fast breathing can trigger, eyelid flutter/face twitch, you don’t remember the seconds, no full-body shaking/falls/tongue biting; occasional headache after. Bring up misconceptions unprompted: tick bite → fear of brain infection; low fevers/aches, tingling, blurry vision, weight loss/night sweats, dark urine. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, say you’re scared and want help but worry about burdening your parent.

confounders: Episodes precipitated by hyperventilation/anxiety at school, leading to concern for panic attacks/psychogenic nonepileptic events
Brief speech arrest without postictal confusion can mimic transient ischemic attack or migraine aura
Facial/perioral twitching may be misattributed to tics or orofacial dyskinesia
Sleep deprivation and stimulant caffeine intake before exams increases event frequency
Use of albuterol during anxiety/wheeze can cause tremor/palpitations, complicating symptom interpretation

acceptable_alternatives: Absence epilepsy with perioral myoclonia

required_evidence: Recurrent brief episodes of impaired awareness with perioral myoclonia (lip/jaw twitching) and behavioral arrest
Event-associated loss of speech (ictal speech arrest) with rapid recovery and minimal postictal confusion
EEG showing generalized 3–4 Hz spike-and-wave or polyspike-and-wave discharges (often precipitated by hyperventilation) consistent with absence seizures with perioral myoclonia
No metabolic derangement on basic labs (glucose/electrolytes) to explain episodic neurologic symptoms
No infectious encephalitis features (fever, meningismus, progressive encephalopathy) and no systemic features suggesting Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia

Ethosuximide 10-15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID, titrate to 20-30 mg/kg/day (alternative if events are purely absence without other generalized seizure types and clinician judges appropriate)
Lamotrigine 0.3 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (or 25 mg PO daily in adolescents/adults) with slow titration per protocol (alternative when valproate not suitable)

Seizure precautions and safety counseling (supervision around water/heights; avoid driving until cleared per local law; school/work accommodations)
Identify and avoid triggers (sleep deprivation, missed doses, alcohol in adults, flashing lights if photosensitive)
Provide a seizure action plan; educate family/caregivers to time events and record video if safe
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 2-4 weeks after initiation/titration; earlier (within 1 week) if seizures persist/worsen or medication adverse effects occur

correct_diagnosis.primary: Iron deficiency anemia (microcytic anemia, presumed chronic blood loss until proven otherwise)
acceptable_alternatives: Thalassemia trait (alpha or beta)

required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with microcytic anemia: low hemoglobin/hematocrit with low MCV

No leukopenia/thrombocytopenia suggesting marrow failure; platelets may be normal or elevated (reactive thrombocytosis)

Review labs to support microcytic anemia and iron deficiency pattern; ensure no features suggesting alternate diagnosis requiring different management.
State working diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia based on CBC (microcytosis) and iron studies; frame need to identify the cause (blood loss vs intake/absorption).
Safety check prior to prescribing iron (non-critical but required).
Critical safety check prior to prescribing: identify interactions (e.g., levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones, bisphosphonates, calcium/antacids, PPIs) and plan dose separation/alternatives.
Repeat/confirm interaction mitigation plan if any interacting meds are present before finalizing prescription (e.g., explicit separation timing).

Critical safety check prior to prescribing: rule out contraindications/precautions (e.g., iron overload disorders, repeated transfusions, active severe infection per local policy, inability to tolerate/absorb oral iron requiring different route).
Initiate oral iron with evidence-based regimen and clear instructions (elemental iron dosing, every-other-day strategy, administration guidance, and duration).
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations, side effects/mitigation, red flags, diet/absorption guidance, stool color counseling, and plan for non-response evaluation.
Arrange reassessment and lab monitoring (repeat Hb/retic response in ~2–4 weeks; ferritin repletion; continued therapy after normalization) and plan further evaluation for bleeding source.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 22-year-old anxious but clear-speaking barista/student. Start by volunteering: “I keep getting chest infections and this cough that just won’t go away,” thick mucus most mornings (yellow/green), getting out of breath on stairs/exercise, and a nose that feels blocked most of the time. If asked about ENT history, share lifelong runny/stuffy nose, frequent sinus pressure/headaches, recurrent ear infections/fullness, mouth-breathing/snoring, multiple pneumonias, breathing trouble as a baby needing oxygen, and worse symptoms in winter. Do not volunteer (unless directly asked): poor sense of smell, constant throat-clearing/postnasal drip, “rattly” chest even when okay, frequent antibiotics since childhood, daytime fatigue from poor sleep. correct_diagnosis.primary: Primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) with bronchiectasis and acute pulmonary exacerbation
acceptable_alternatives: Primary ciliary dyskinesia with acute infective exacerbation of bronchiectasis
PCD with acute bacterial lower respiratory tract infection
required_evidence: History consistent with impaired mucociliary clearance (chronic wet cough and recurrent sino-pulmonary infections since childhood)
Evidence of bronchiectasis and/or chronic airway disease on prior imaging or clinical history (recurrent productive cough, frequent antibiotic courses)

At least one supportive feature: chronic rhinosinusitis/otitis media, neonatal respiratory distress at term, laterality defect (situs inversus/heterotaxy), infertility history, or prior abnormal ciliary testing
Current exacerbation features: increased cough/sputum volume or purulence, increased dyspnea/wheeze, fever or malaise, and/or inflammatory marker elevation (CRP)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal to mildly elevated; may show leukocytosis with neutrophilia during bacterial exacerbation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly low if chronic inflammation
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.CRP: Mild to moderately elevated during acute exacerbation; can be normal in mild/viral exacerbations
expected_lab_results.Basic Metabolic Panel - Creatinine: Typically normal; needed to guide antibiotic dosing if renally cleared agents are used
expected_lab_results.Serum IgG: Expected normal in isolated PCD; low IgG would prompt evaluation for concomitant immunodeficiency contributing to recurrent infections

medications: Doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 7-10 days
Azithromycin 500 mg PO on day 1, then 250 mg PO daily on days 2-5 (alternative to doxycycline if clinically appropriate)
non_pharmacological: Airway clearance therapy escalation: chest physiotherapy/oscillatory PEP or vest therapy at least 2-4 times daily during exacerbation
Hydration and humidification; consider nebulized hypertonic saline if already part of regimen and tolerated
Obtain sputum culture (including prior colonization history) when available to tailor antibiotics; consider Pseudomonas coverage if prior colonization/bronchiectasis severity suggests
Assess oxygenation and clinical severity; consider chest imaging if severe symptoms, hemoptysis, pleuritic pain, or poor response to therapy
Vaccinations up to date (influenza annually, pneumococcal per guidelines)
Referral/coordination with pulmonology/PCD center; consider ENT follow-up for chronic rhinosinusitis/otitis
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48-72 hours (sooner if worsening); routine follow-up within 1-2 weeks to confirm symptom resolution and review culture results

must_include: PCD is a genetic condition where airway cilia do not clear mucus well, leading to recurrent sinus/chest infections and bronchiectasis
Current symptoms represent an acute exacerbation/infection on top of chronic airway disease
Need to watch for red flags (worsening shortness of breath, high fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, low oxygen, dehydration)
Airway clearance is a core therapy and should be increased during exacerbations
Antibiotic choice should avoid amoxicillin-clavulanate due to prior urticarial rash; alternatives and duration explained
Plan to tailor antibiotics based on sputum culture/previous organisms and to reassess response within 48-72 hours
Review allergy history and what to do if signs of allergic reaction occur
Discuss expected course, side effects (e.g., GI upset, photosensitivity with doxycycline; QT risk with azithromycin in predisposed patients)
Shared decision-making on antibiotic option and follow-up plan
Importance of adherence to daily airway clearance even when well; increase frequency when sick
Infection prevention: hand hygiene, vaccines, avoiding smoke exposure
When to seek urgent care and how to contact the care team for worsening symptoms

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current exacerbation history and severity to guide urgency, diagnostics, and therapy in PCD.

Clarify baseline PCD status (usual sputum, airway clearance routine, prior bronchiectasis, prior organisms, prior exacerbation frequency) and comorbidities (asthma/COPD, cardiac disease, pregnancy).
Critical before prescribing: confirm beta-lactam allergy details (urticarial rash to amoxicillin-clavulanate), severity, timing, and other antibiotic tolerances.
Non-critical contraindication check before ordering: order sputum culture (and Gram stain if available) to tailor antibiotics to prior organisms in PCD/bronchiectasis.
Consider basic assessment labs if system supports (CBC, CRP) and pulse oximetry if available; helps severity assessment and follow-up.
Review available results (especially sputum culture history if returned) to inform empiric choice and subsequent adjustment.
State working diagnosis: acute infective pulmonary exacerbation in primary ciliary dyskinesia/bronchiectasis; note differential if symptoms suggest pneumonia, PE, asthma flare.
Critical before prescribing: check interactions with candidate antibiotics (e.g., macrolides with QT-prolonging agents, warfarin; doxycycline with isotretinoin/antacids).
Critical before prescribing: ensure no conflicting chronic meds (antiarrhythmics, SSRIs with QT risk, anticoagulants) and counsel accordingly.
Initiate empiric antibiotic avoiding amoxicillin-clavulanate; select an appropriate alternative and duration; plan to adjust based on culture and clinical response within 48–72 hours.
Explain diagnosis and acute-on-chronic nature; reinforce airway clearance escalation; discuss red flags and when to seek urgent care.
Explain treatment options/side effects and allergy precautions; shared decision-making on antibiotic choice; infection prevention and adherence to airway clearance.
Arrange reassessment in 48–72 hours (earlier if worse) and plan for culture review and antibiotic adjustment; document return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying you keep getting brutal one-sided headaches around your left eye/temple that come out of nowhere. Volunteer: severe stabbing pain, comes on fast, so intense you can’t sit still, occurs in clusters for a few weeks then months of no symptoms, and during attacks your left eye waters and turns red. If asked, add: left nostril stuffy/runny, mild left eyelid droop, attacks last 45–90 minutes, often wake you 1–2 hours after sleep, 1–3/day during a cluster, alcohol triggers during bad stretches, no significant nausea, and light/noise don’t bother you much. Keep hidden unless specifically explored: restlessness/panic with pacing, same time each day, totally normal between attacks, and your dad had “cluster headaches.” Be reluctant about lifestyle: only admit increased smoking and heavy energy drinks if asked directly; you’re embarrassed. Express misconceptions: worry it’s sinus or dental because it feels behind the eye and jaw/teeth ache; ibuprofen barely helps so you fear something serious. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and stay engaged. Bring up concerns about oxygen cost/insurance and missing work; ask for a plan that minimizes time away.

medications: Hydroxyurea 500 mg PO once daily
Sumatriptan 6 mg SC as needed at headache onset (max 2 doses/24 h)
Oxygen 100% via non-rebreather 12-15 L/min for 15-20 minutes as needed for acute attacks
Verapamil ER 240 mg PO once daily
confounders: Unilateral autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, nasal congestion) can mimic sinusitis/dental pathology
Active tobacco use (1 pack/day) and alcohol as a trigger during cluster periods
Medication-related cytopenias from hydroxyurea may limit use of corticosteroid bridging and complicate evaluation for infection
Recent viral URI symptoms increasing concern for secondary headache/infection despite normal inflammatory markers

acceptable_alternatives: Episodic cluster headache
Chronic cluster headache
required_evidence: Severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes
Attacks occur in clusters (e.g., 1 every other day up to 8/day) with circadian/circannual pattern
Ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms during attacks (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis)
Marked restlessness or agitation during attacks
No better explanation by secondary headache disorder; screen for red flags (sudden thunderclap onset, new focal neurologic deficit, fever/meningismus, new headache >50, cancer/immunosuppression)

expected_lab_results.CBC_ANC: Within reference range; ensure not neutropenic (hydroxyurea can lower ANC)
expected_lab_results.CBC_Hemoglobin: Within reference range unless hydroxyurea-related anemia
expected_lab_results.CBC_Platelets: Within reference range unless hydroxyurea-related thrombocytopenia
expected_lab_results.CMP_Creatinine: Within reference range (needed before/for lithium or other renally cleared options if considered)

medications: Oxygen 100% 12-15 L/min non-rebreather mask at onset for 15-20 minutes PRN attack
Sumatriptan 6 mg subcutaneous at onset; may repeat once after 1 hour (max 12 mg/24 h)
Verapamil 80 mg oral three times daily; titrate every 7-14 days as needed for prevention (with ECG monitoring)
Prednisone 60 mg oral daily for 5 days then taper by 10 mg every 2-3 days as transitional therapy
non_pharmacological: Avoid alcohol during cluster period (common trigger)
Maintain consistent sleep schedule; avoid sleep deprivation
Headache diary (timing, triggers, response to abortives)
Offer referral to neurology/headache specialist for preventive titration and consideration of alternatives (e.g., galcanezumab, occipital nerve block) if refractory
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks to assess response and adverse effects; sooner (48-72 hours) if attacks remain frequent/severe despite abortive therapy

must_include: Cluster headache is a primary headache disorder with short, very severe unilateral attacks
Autonomic symptoms and restlessness are typical and help distinguish from migraine
Familial tendency can occur; not caused by penicillin allergy
Two-part plan: fast abortive treatment (oxygen, triptan) plus preventive therapy (verapamil) and possible short steroid bridge
How to use oxygen correctly (non-rebreather, high flow, early at onset)
Triptan limits and when not to use (heart disease/uncontrolled hypertension/ergot use within 24h)
Review current hydroxyurea use and emphasize need for CBC monitoring due to myelosuppression risk
Discuss potential verapamil side effects (constipation, dizziness, bradycardia) and need for ECG monitoring with dose increases
Safety-net: seek urgent care for new neurologic deficits, thunderclap onset, fever/neck stiffness, or atypical/worsening pattern
Avoid alcohol during active cluster periods
Sleep regularity and trigger tracking
Adherence to preventive medication even when pain-free between attacks during the cluster period

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit headache features to confirm cluster phenotype (severe unilateral orbital/temporal pain, 15–180 min, frequency, circadian pattern), associated ipsilateral autonomic symptoms, restlessness, and rule out red flags/secondary causes.

Assess cardiovascular history and risk factors relevant to triptan use (CAD, stroke/TIA, uncontrolled HTN), pregnancy status if relevant, and current meds (including ergot use) for contraindications/interactions.
State diagnosis of (familial) cluster headache based on classic clinical pattern; clarify why migraine and allergy-related etiologies are less likely.
Perform required critical safety check prior to prescribing any abortive/preventive therapy; document allergies and reaction types.
Perform required critical drug interaction check before prescribing (e.g., triptan with ergot within 24h; verapamil with other rate-limiting agents; consider hydroxyurea context).
Confirm no contraindications to proposed therapies (oxygen safety, triptan contraindications, verapamil precautions) and assess baseline vitals/symptoms (bradycardia, syncope).
If new meds are added after initial review (e.g., steroid bridge), re-check interactions as needed to maintain safety compliance.

Order baseline/monitoring tests relevant to current hydroxyurea (CBC) and preventive therapy planning; ECG is typically required for verapamil initiation/titration (may be represented as an order if supported).

Initiate two-part treatment plan: abortive therapy (high-flow oxygen; triptan if eligible) plus preventive therapy (verapamil) and consider short steroid bridge; include clear dosing/use instructions and limits.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment (oxygen technique, triptan limits/contraindications), address concerns (hydroxyurea monitoring, verapamil adverse effects/ECG), lifestyle education, and safety-net guidance.
Arrange close follow-up for response assessment and verapamil titration with ECG monitoring; ensure CBC monitoring plan for hydroxyurea is reinforced.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan adherence, and return precautions.

nl_assertions: Agent confirms key cluster headache characteristics: very severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain lasting 15–180 minutes, occurring in bouts with multiple attacks/day, with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, ptosis/miosis) and/or marked restlessness/agitation.

Agent explicitly distinguishes cluster headache from migraine by emphasizing short duration, extreme intensity, prominent cranial autonomic symptoms, and restlessness (rather than preference for lying still).
Agent addresses familial tendency accurately (can occur) and clearly states it is not caused by penicillin allergy.
Agent screens for red flags and states safety-net instructions: urgent evaluation for thunderclap onset, new focal neurologic deficits, fever/neck stiffness, or atypical/worsening pattern.
Before any prescribing, agent completes all three required safety checks: allergy_check, contraindication_check, and drug_interaction_check; omission of any is a critical failure.

Agent assesses triptan contraindications (ischemic heart disease, prior stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension) and checks for ergot use within the prior 24 hours; if present, agent avoids triptan and offers alternatives (oxygen-only and/or non-triptan strategies).

Agent provides a two-part treatment plan: rapid abortive therapy (high-flow oxygen and/or triptan) plus preventive therapy (verapamil) and may include a short corticosteroid bridge while preventive takes effect.

Agent teaches correct oxygen use: 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask at high flow (typically 12–15 L/min or higher per local protocol), started early at attack onset, for ~15–20 minutes, and not via nasal cannula.

Agent communicates triptan limits (e.g., maximum daily dosing per product, avoid repeated doses beyond guidance) and reiterates when not to use triptans (cardiovascular disease/uncontrolled hypertension/ergot within 24h).
Agent reviews current hydroxyurea use and emphasizes CBC monitoring due to myelosuppression risk; advises to follow prescriber’s monitoring schedule and to seek care for infection signs.

Agent discusses verapamil side effects (constipation, dizziness, bradycardia/hypotension, edema) and the need for baseline and follow-up ECG monitoring with dose increases to detect conduction abnormalities.

Agent includes lifestyle counseling: avoid alcohol during active cluster periods, maintain regular sleep, track triggers/attack timing, and adhere to preventive medication even when pain-free between attacks during the cluster period.
Agent’s plan is clinically coherent and avoids unsafe combinations (e.g., triptan with ergot within 24h; verapamil without ECG monitoring plan when titrating).

general_concerns: Thinks the illness is caught from eating pork or from pigs directly, not from mosquito bites.
Believes mosquitoes only bite at night indoors, so playing outside at dusk near fields 'can’t be the cause.'
Thinks vaccines are only needed for travel and that 'local kids don’t need them.'
Believes fever with confusion is just 'bad flu' and will pass with herbal remedies and rest.
Thinks seizures mean the child is 'possessed' or has a curse, so hospital treatment may not help.

Fears missing work during harvest will threaten the family’s income more than the illness itself.
Believes diagnostic tests (lumbar puncture/CT) are unnecessary 'money-making' and wants only cheap medicines.
Assumes referral to a larger hospital means unaffordable transport and hidden fees.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy. Act evasive and vague; answer briefly, often saying “I don’t know” or “maybe.” Start by volunteering: 2 days of high fever, very bad nonstop headache, confusion since this morning, nausea with 2 vomits, extreme tiredness and wanting to sleep. Do NOT mention other symptoms unless directly asked. If asked about neck/head: admit stiff neck and light hurting your eyes. If asked about seizures/jerks: describe one shaking episode with arm jerking and blanking out ~1 minute; also brief muscle twitches. If asked about behavior: family says you say strange things, sometimes don’t know where you are; unsteady walking. If asked about chills/urine: chills/sweats and peeing less today. Keep hidden unless specifically asked: pauses in breathing during sleep with gasping, racing heart, hot/cold swings, bluish lips, coughing when swallowing water, drifting off mid-sentence. Resist admitting bedwetting, bowel accident during shaking, and scary visions—deny at first, then reluctantly admit if clinician is gentle and assures privacy. Misattribute symptoms to food poisoning, worst migraine, heatstroke, or flu/COVID. Express worry about upfront cash, missing harvest work, and tests being “money-making”; ask for cheap medicine only. If met with empathy, soften slightly but remain anxious about costs.

medications: BiPAP nocturnal (IPAP 14 cmH2O / EPAP 6 cmH2O) nightly
Acetazolamide 250 mg PO twice daily
Hydrocortisone 10 mg PO every morning and 5 mg PO every evening
Clonidine 0.1 mg PO at bedtime
Levetiracetam 500 mg IV every 12 hours
Acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO/IV every 6 hours as needed for fever
Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV every 8 hours
Ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV every 12 hours
Vancomycin 15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours
comorbidities: Rapid-onset obesity with hypothalamic dysfunction, hypoventilation, and autonomic dysregulation (ROHHAD syndrome)
Obstructive sleep apnea/hypoventilation requiring nocturnal noninvasive ventilation
confounders: Chronic hypoventilation from ROHHAD causing baseline hypercapnia and low respiratory rate, complicating assessment of encephalopathy and respiratory failure
Autonomic dysregulation (episodic hypertension/tachycardia, temperature instability) mimicking sepsis or increased intracranial pressure physiology
Recent mosquito exposure during travel to rural East/Southeast Asia with incomplete vaccination history; flavivirus serology cross-reactivity can complicate interpretation
Empiric acyclovir/antibiotics started prior to CSF culture finalization, potentially obscuring bacterial meningitis evaluation

acceptable_alternatives: Viral encephalitis (arboviral encephalitis)
Acute encephalitis syndrome (AES) with suspected flavivirus
West Nile virus encephalitis
Herpes simplex virus encephalitis
Acute bacterial meningitis/meningoencephalitis
Cerebral malaria
required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with altered mental status and/or seizures and neurologic deficits consistent with encephalitis
Epidemiologic risk for JE (residence/travel in endemic area, rural/agricultural exposure, mosquito exposure, lack of vaccination or incomplete vaccination)
CSF profile consistent with viral encephalitis (lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein, normal glucose) OR neuroimaging consistent with JE (bilateral thalamic/basal ganglia involvement)
Exclusion/coverage of immediately treatable causes (empiric HSV coverage started; bacterial meningitis considered/covered until ruled out)
Confirmatory testing planned/obtained: JE virus IgM in CSF and/or serum (MAC-ELISA) with confirmatory neutralization if available

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (often 8–15 x10^9/L); may show mild leukocytosis
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal; can be mildly low in systemic viral illness but not a defining feature
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low (mild hyponatremia possible due to SIADH in CNS infection)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): May be low-normal; can decrease with seizures or respiratory compensation

medications: Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h (use ideal body weight; adjust for renal function) until HSV encephalitis is excluded
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (or 2 g IV q24h if meningitis not strongly suspected) as empiric coverage for bacterial meningitis/meningoencephalitis until ruled out
Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (target AUC-guided dosing per local protocol) until bacterial meningitis is excluded (especially if pneumococcal risk)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h for 4 days only if bacterial meningitis is strongly suspected and given before/with first antibiotic dose; discontinue if meningitis ruled out
Levetiracetam 1-2 g IV load, then 500-1500 mg IV/PO q12h for seizure control/prophylaxis if seizures occur
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/PR q6h PRN fever/pain (max 3-4 g/day depending on liver risk)
non_pharmacological: Admit to ICU/step-down for close neurologic monitoring; frequent neuro checks
Airway protection and aspiration precautions; intubate if GCS low or unable to protect airway
Seizure precautions; treat active seizures per status epilepticus protocol
Manage raised intracranial pressure if present: head-of-bed 30 degrees, maintain normoxia/normocapnia, avoid hypotonic fluids; consider hypertonic saline/mannitol per ICU protocol
Maintain euvolemia; correct hyponatremia cautiously (evaluate for SIADH)
Infection control per standard precautions; mosquito avoidance counseling for contacts/travelers (repellent, bed nets, protective clothing)
Diagnostic/consult steps: lumbar puncture (if no contraindication), brain MRI preferred (or CT if needed), EEG if seizures/NCSE concern; notify public health/infectious diseases
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 hours after initiation of empiric therapy; daily inpatient reassessment with de-escalation once diagnostics return; outpatient neurology/ID follow-up within 2-4 weeks after discharge for neurocognitive sequelae and rehabilitation planning

must_include: This is an inflammation of the brain (encephalitis) likely caused by a mosquito-borne virus; Japanese encephalitis is suspected based on symptoms and exposure risk
Illness can be severe with seizures, confusion, and possible long-term neurologic problems
Confirmation requires specific testing (JE IgM in CSF/serum); other causes like HSV and bacterial meningitis must be treated/ruled out urgently
No proven antiviral therapy specifically cures Japanese encephalitis; treatment is supportive (airway, fluids, fever control, seizure management, intracranial pressure management)
Empiric medications are started immediately to cover treatable, dangerous alternatives (acyclovir for HSV; antibiotics for bacterial meningitis) and will be stopped if tests rule them out
Need for ICU-level monitoring may be required depending on mental status and breathing
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and potential for neurologic sequelae; emphasize early supportive care improves safety
Review allergies: amoxicillin causes hives (non-anaphylaxis) and latex causes contact dermatitis; plan to avoid latex products and choose antibiotics appropriately
Explain key warning signs to report immediately (worsening confusion, seizures, breathing difficulty)
Mosquito bite prevention (DEET/picaridin, long sleeves, bed nets, avoiding dusk/dawn exposure in endemic areas)
Vaccination counseling for future risk (JE vaccine for travelers/at-risk populations) and travel health planning
Rehabilitation needs after recovery (physical/occupational/speech therapy) and follow-up for cognitive or behavioral changes

Order urgent diagnostic workup for encephalitis/meningitis including CBC/CMP, blood cultures, glucose, coagulation as needed, and infectious testing plan.

Order neuro/infectious diagnostics: lumbar puncture with CSF studies (cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain/culture), CSF HSV PCR, and JE IgM in CSF/serum; consider other arboviral testing per region; order neuroimaging (CT/MRI) when indicated prior to LP.
Noncritical but expected before prescribing; screen for interactions with planned empiric agents (e.g., acyclovir nephrotoxicity risk with other nephrotoxins; antibiotic interactions).

Start immediate empiric therapy for treatable life-threatening alternatives while awaiting results: IV acyclovir for HSV encephalitis and empiric antibiotics for bacterial meningitis per age/risk; add adjuncts/supportive meds as clinically indicated (antipyretics, anticonvulsants).
Ensure allergy check is explicitly completed before finalizing antibiotic selection (e.g., amoxicillin hives; avoid/choose alternatives or cephalosporin strategy based on severity).
Review initial labs/CSF/imaging results to refine differential, assess for bacterial meningitis/HSV, and evaluate for JE likelihood.
Synthesize presentation and testing: suspected Japanese encephalitis based on exposure and encephalitis syndrome while continuing to rule out/treat HSV and bacterial meningitis until excluded.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis uncertainty and severity; explain supportive care and empiric coverage; address allergies/latex avoidance and warning signs; counsel mosquito prevention, future vaccination, and rehab needs.
Arrange ICU/ward monitoring and follow-up planning (neurology/infectious disease, rehab services, cognitive/behavioral follow-up) based on clinical course.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, urgent treatments initiated, and patient/family understanding assessed.

nl_assertions: Agent recognizes encephalitis as a medical emergency and prioritizes stabilization/urgent evaluation rather than outpatient management.
Agent explicitly states that Japanese encephalitis is mosquito-borne viral encephalitis suspected from symptoms and exposure risk, and that confirmation requires JE IgM testing in CSF/serum.

Agent states that HSV encephalitis and bacterial meningitis must be urgently treated/ruled out and initiates empiric acyclovir plus appropriate empiric antibiotics without waiting for confirmatory JE testing.

Agent provides warning signs requiring immediate reporting (worsening confusion, seizures, breathing difficulty) and checks understanding.
Agent includes prevention counseling: mosquito bite prevention measures and JE vaccination counseling for future travel/at-risk exposure, plus rehabilitation/follow-up for neurologic sequelae.
Agent avoids overclaiming certainty of JE diagnosis before test results and avoids recommending unproven JE-specific antivirals as curative therapy.

minimal_paths.description: Emergency encephalitis pathway: rapid assessment, required safety checks, immediate empiric HSV/bacterial coverage, targeted CSF/serum testing including JE IgM, then diagnosis/education.
steps: ASK: encephalitis/meningitis symptoms and onset; seizure/airway red flags
ASK: exposure risks (mosquito, travel, rural/pig/bird), vaccine status; alternative causes (HSV, bacterial meningitis risks)
ORDER_LAB: neuro/CSF plan including LP with CSF studies, CSF HSV PCR, JE IgM CSF/serum; imaging when indicated
PRESCRIBE: IV acyclovir + empiric meningitis antibiotics tailored to allergy history; supportive meds as needed
DIAGNOSE: suspected JE while continuing empiric coverage until alternatives excluded
EDUCATE: diagnosis, treatment/supportive care, allergy/latex plan, warning signs, prevention/vaccine/rehab

minimal_paths.description: If signs of elevated ICP or focal deficits are suspected, obtain neuroimaging prior to LP while still initiating empiric therapy and JE testing.
steps: ASK: red flags for elevated ICP/focal deficit and exposure history
ORDER_LAB: urgent neuroimaging prior to LP due to concern for elevated ICP; plan LP after imaging
PRESCRIBE: start IV acyclovir + empiric meningitis antibiotics immediately (do not wait for LP)
ORDER_LAB: LP after imaging with CSF studies, CSF HSV PCR, JE IgM CSF/serum

steps: ORDER_LAB (expanded infectious/autoimmune panel including JE IgM CSF/serum and HSV PCR)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs contraindication checks explicitly before ordering LP/imaging and before prescribing, documenting rationale (e.g., anticoagulation/bleeding risk for LP; renal function for acyclovir).

Agent explicitly states why an ectopic source is suspected: elevated plasma GHRH favors a tumor outside the pituitary rather than a typical pituitary adenoma.
Agent communicates that the source tumor must be localized with targeted imaging and treated when possible, and does not imply medical therapy alone is always definitive.
Agent states treatment goals: tumor control/removal when feasible and normalization of IGF-1 to reduce long-term morbidity and mortality.
Agent accurately describes treatment classes: somatostatin analogs reduce GH secretion; pegvisomant blocks GH action to normalize IGF-1; cabergoline may be adjunct in selected cases.
Agent provides a monitoring plan including repeat IGF-1 to guide titration and glucose monitoring because disease and therapies can affect blood sugar.

Agent discusses key adverse effects: somatostatin analogs (GI upset, gallstones, glucose changes), pegvisomant (liver enzyme elevation), cabergoline (nausea, orthostasis, rare valvulopathy at higher cumulative doses).
Agent clarifies penicillin allergy relevance: avoid penicillin-class antibiotics if needed; current endocrine therapies are not penicillins, while still documenting allergy details.
Agent performs critical safety checks (allergy and contraindications) before any prescribing action; missing either is a critical failure.
Agent uses shared decision-making to select therapy based on tumor status/localization plan, biochemical severity, glucose tolerance, and patient preferences.

Agent provides lifestyle and risk-reduction education: nutrition strategies for poor appetite/weight maintenance, glucose-friendly diet/activity as tolerated, screening for sleep apnea and cardiovascular risk factors, and adherence to follow-up labs/imaging to reduce risks (cardiac disease, diabetes, sleep apnea, colon polyps).
Agent avoids unsafe claims (e.g., guaranteeing cure, ignoring monitoring needs, or advising stopping diabetes medications without a plan).

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Often normal to mildly elevated (possible secondary erythrocytosis if chronic hypoxemia); anemia would worsen dyspnea and should be addressed
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Often normal to mildly elevated if chronic hypoxemia; otherwise may be normal
expected_lab_results.WBC: Typically normal (marked elevation would increase suspicion for pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Usually normal (may be low if advanced right heart failure with congestion; otherwise normal)
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Normal; hyponatremia may indicate advanced heart failure/right-sided congestion and worse prognosis

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for rickets and hereditary vitamin D pathway disorders: onset, growth, bone pain/weakness, fractures, deformities, diet/sun exposure, prior vitamin D response, family history/consanguinity, renal/hepatic disease, medications, and red-flag symptoms.

Order diagnostic labs to distinguish vitamin D-dependent rickets from nutritional deficiency, renal phosphate wasting, CKD, and skeletal dysplasias: Ca, phosphate, ALP, PTH, 25(OH)D, 1,25(OH)2D, creatinine/eGFR, Mg, urine Ca/Cr, urine phosphate (or TRP/TmP-GFR), and consider VBG/CO2 if RTA suspected.

Interpret biochemical pattern consistent with VDDR (e.g., elevated ALP, elevated PTH, low/normal Ca, low phosphate; 25(OH)D not severely low; and 1,25(OH)2D low in VDDR1 or high in VDDR2) and assess baseline renal status and hypercalciuria risk.
Make and communicate working diagnosis (vitamin D-dependent rickets, likely type 1 if 1,25(OH)2D is low with normal 25(OH)D) and document differential reasoning.
Safety check before prescribing calcitriol and calcium products (rare but required): allergies to excipients, prior reactions to vitamin D analogs or calcium salts.
Review interactions that increase hypercalcemia/hypercalciuria risk or alter calcitriol effect (e.g., thiazides, digoxin, antacids with Mg, anticonvulsants, glucocorticoids, cholestyramine).

Initiate appropriate therapy: active vitamin D (calcitriol) rather than cholecalciferol alone for suspected VDDR1; add calcium supplementation initially; specify monitoring plan and titration targets based on Ca/PTH/ALP and urine Ca/Cr.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment rationale, risks and monitoring, lifestyle/diet guidance, and urgent return precautions for hypercalcemia.

Arrange close follow-up and repeat labs (serum Ca/phos/ALP/PTH, urine Ca/Cr, creatinine) within 1–2 weeks after starting or changing dose, then spaced out; consider renal ultrasound for nephrocalcinosis surveillance.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, adherence plan, and follow-up logistics.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male auto-rickshaw driver with middle-school education. Be vague and evasive; answer briefly unless asked directly. Start by saying your hips and lower back have hurt for years and lately you can barely walk without sitting. Volunteer: deep aching pain in both hips worse with walking/standing, slowly worsening low back pain, you tire quickly and need frequent breaks, and you’ve always been shorter than most in your family.

If asked, add: morning hip stiffness that eases after moving, trouble squatting/cross-legged, “waddly” gait/limp, pain into buttocks/thighs (not sharp electric), knee ache on stairs, told as a teen your spine was “curved,” hip pain since late teens, OTC pills only help a little.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: unsteadiness/holding railings, legs feel weak/hips might give way, can’t fully straighten hips, difficulty rising from low chairs, uneven shoe wear.

Resist discussing intimacy pain, urine leakage with cough/laugh, and low mood—minimize or deflect unless asked sensitively.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s inflammatory arthritis or sciatica; tried gluten-free; blame overuse. Be anxious about “genetic” labels, costs, and losing wages; push for a quick cure. If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly and share one extra detail.

medications: naproxen 500 mg PO twice daily as needed for pain (with food)
omeprazole 20 mg PO once daily
acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed (max 3 g/day)
cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO once daily
comorbidities: early-onset osteoarthritis of hips and knees secondary to skeletal dysplasia
chronic mechanical low back pain with reduced spinal mobility
vitamin D deficiency with secondary hyperparathyroidism
confounders: mechanical back pain and stiffness can mimic inflammatory spondyloarthropathy despite normal ESR/CRP and negative HLA-B27
vitamin D deficiency with secondary hyperparathyroidism can contribute to diffuse bone pain and proximal muscle weakness, complicating attribution of symptoms to dysplasia/osteoarthritis
overweight increases joint loading and accelerates degenerative symptoms, obscuring baseline skeletal dysplasia-related pain
regular NSAID use may partially mask inflammatory symptoms and can complicate assessment of pain severity and function

acceptable_alternatives: Spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia (SEMD) spectrum disorder
Spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia with early-onset osteoarthritis (Handigodu phenotype)
Spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia, unspecified type (if Handigodu type not fully confirmed)
required_evidence: Childhood- or early-adult onset disproportionate short stature and progressive hip/knee pain with early osteoarthritis
Radiographic pattern consistent with SEMD: platyspondyly and epiphyseal/metaphyseal changes (especially hips; femoral head/neck dysplasia) with degenerative changes out of proportion to age
Non-inflammatory profile: absence of systemic inflammatory symptoms and normal/low inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) supporting degenerative rather than inflammatory arthritis
Family history and/or genetic evaluation consistent with inherited skeletal dysplasia (or referral planned if not yet performed)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no anemia of chronic disease expected in isolated skeletal dysplasia/degenerative OA).
expected_lab_results.ESR: Normal or mildly elevated; should not show a sustained inflammatory pattern.
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Creatini: Normal (baseline needed prior to NSAID use).
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - eGFR: Normal (>=60 mL/min/1.73 m²) expected; confirm prior to NSAIDs.
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - AST: Normal (baseline needed prior to acetaminophen/NSAID use).

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours with food as needed for pain (use lowest effective dose; avoid if eGFR <60, GI bleed history, or uncontrolled hypertension)
Diclofenac 1% gel apply 2-4 g topically to affected joint up to 4 times daily (as an alternative/adjunct to oral NSAIDs)
non_pharmacological: Referral to orthopedics for assessment of hip/knee deformity and early osteoarthritis; discuss timing of joint-preserving procedures vs arthroplasty depending on severity and function
Physical therapy focusing on hip/core strengthening, range of motion, gait training, and low-impact conditioning (cycling, swimming)
Activity modification: avoid high-impact loading; pacing strategies; consider assistive devices (cane/walker) if gait instability or pain-limited ambulation
Weight optimization and nutrition counseling if overweight to reduce joint load
Genetics referral (or skeletal dysplasia clinic) for confirmation, counseling, and family planning discussion
Baseline and periodic assessment of function and pain (e.g., WOMAC/HOOS/KOOS) and fall risk screening
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess pain control, function, and medication tolerability in 4-6 weeks; sooner (1-2 weeks) if starting regular NSAID use or if symptoms worsen.

must_include: This is a genetic skeletal dysplasia affecting the spine and ends of long bones, which predisposes to early joint degeneration (especially hips/knees)
The condition is not an infection and usually not an inflammatory arthritis; labs are expected to be non-inflammatory
Confirmatory steps: review prior imaging, consider targeted skeletal dysplasia/genetic evaluation, and assess severity/complications
Goals: reduce pain, preserve mobility, prevent disability, and plan timely orthopedic interventions
Stepwise analgesia: start with acetaminophen/topical NSAID; oral NSAID only if kidney function and GI/cardiovascular risks acceptable
When to seek urgent care: sudden severe joint pain, fever, inability to bear weight, new neurologic symptoms, or signs of GI bleeding
Discuss prognosis: symptoms may progress due to early osteoarthritis; many patients benefit from PT and may eventually require orthopedic surgery
Discuss inheritance/family implications and offer genetics counseling
Acknowledge chronic pain impact and offer supportive resources (PT, pain management if refractory)
Low-impact exercise and strengthening are preferred; avoid repetitive high-impact activities
Joint protection strategies and assistive devices can reduce pain and improve safety
Medication safety: avoid exceeding acetaminophen daily maximum; take NSAIDs with food and avoid combining multiple NSAIDs

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history: pain pattern (hips/knees/spine), functional limits, neurologic symptoms, red flags (fever, acute swelling, inability to bear weight), prior imaging/genetic workup, meds tried, comorbidities (renal/GI/CV), pregnancy status if relevant, and family history.

Order baseline safety and rule-out labs consistent with non-inflammatory expectation and analgesic safety: CBC, CMP/creatinine, LFTs; consider ESR/CRP only if inflammatory/infectious concern; consider vitamin D if bone health addressed.
Review labs to confirm non-inflammatory profile and ensure renal/hepatic function supports acetaminophen/NSAID choices.

If not already available, request/review prior imaging (pelvis/hip/knee/spine radiographs) or order appropriate X-rays to assess degenerative changes and dysplasia severity; consider referral-oriented testing (not as routine lab) such as genetic evaluation planning.
Interpret imaging (or documented findings) consistent with spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia and early osteoarthritis; identify complications (hip deformity, severe OA, spine stenosis/spondylosis).

State working diagnosis: spondyloepimetaphyseal dysplasia, Handigodu type with secondary early degenerative joint disease; document differential exclusions (infection/inflammatory arthritis) based on history and labs.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (genetic skeletal dysplasia; non-infectious/non-inflammatory labs; confirmatory steps), explain treatment goals and stepwise analgesia, address concerns (prognosis, genetics counseling, chronic pain resources), and lifestyle education (low-impact exercise, joint protection, medication safety).
Critical safety check before prescribing any analgesic (acetaminophen/NSAIDs) or adjuvant medication.
Critical safety check before prescribing to avoid interactions (e.g., NSAIDs with anticoagulants/antiplatelets, ACEi/ARB+diuretic, SSRIs, steroids).

Confirm contraindication-relevant history before prescribing: CKD, peptic ulcer/GI bleed, uncontrolled HTN, heart failure, CAD, liver disease, asthma/NSAID sensitivity, pregnancy, alcohol use, and current OTC NSAID use.

Provide stepwise analgesia plan: acetaminophen and/or topical NSAID first; oral NSAID only if renal function and GI/CV risks acceptable; include dosing limits and precautions; consider gastroprotection if indicated; avoid duplicate NSAIDs.
Arrange follow-up to reassess pain/function, review imaging/genetics referrals, monitor medication tolerance/renal function if NSAID used, and escalate to PT/orthopedics as needed.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, red-flag return precautions, and plan.

Before any prescribing, the agent performs an allergy check and a contraindication check; failure to do so is a critical error.
The agent avoids recommending aspirin, NSAIDs, or intramuscular injections without hematology guidance.
If any red-flag bleeding symptoms are present, the agent prioritizes urgent escalation rather than routine outpatient management.

minimal_paths.description: Routine counseling visit for a patient with hemophilia A on emicizumab; no active bleeding; needs education and safe analgesia advice.
must_collect: history of bleeding episodes and red-flag symptoms
allergies and prior adverse reactions (including NSAID-related bleeding)
planned procedures (dental/surgery) and need for HTC coordination
steps: ASK: reason for visit, hemophilia type/severity, current prophylaxis and adherence
ASK: screen for red-flag bleeding symptoms and recent trauma
CHECK_ALLERGY: confirm allergies and prior reactions, including NSAID-associated bleeding
ASK: contraindication-relevant history and full medication reconciliation
CHECK_INTERACTION: review for interactions with current meds/hemostatic agents
DIAGNOSE: hemophilia A on emicizumab prophylaxis; no acute bleed
EDUCATE: diagnosis + emicizumab lab caveat (aPTT/one-stage FVIII misleading)
EDUCATE: treatment expectations and emergency plan/when to seek urgent care
PRESCRIBE: acetaminophen PRN if pain; avoid NSAIDs
EDUCATE: lifestyle/dental/medical alert guidance; confirm understanding of schedule
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: HTC follow-up and return precautions

minimal_paths.description: Patient reports possible bleeding symptoms; agent triages for severity and directs to HTC/urgent care per emergency plan while reinforcing emicizumab limitations and analgesic safety.
must_collect: presence/absence of head injury or neurologic symptoms
current emicizumab adherence and last dose
steps: ASK: focused bleeding history and red-flag screen (head/neck/neurologic/uncontrolled bleeding)
ASK: medication reconciliation and contraindication-relevant history
DIAGNOSE: suspected bleeding in hemophilia A on emicizumab
EDUCATE: urgent escalation instructions (HTC/emergency plan; ER triggers) and testing caveat on emicizumab
PRESCRIBE: acetaminophen only if appropriate; avoid NSAIDs
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: same-day HTC contact or ED referral depending on red flags

acceptable_variants.description: Performs contraindication screening as part of the initial history-taking before formal safety tool checks, but still completes CHECK_ALLERGY and contraindication assessment before PRESCRIBE.
steps: ASK (includes contraindication-focused questions and med reconciliation)

acceptable_variants.description: Omits labs entirely (appropriate for L0 education-focused scenario) and emphasizes HTC coordination and safety-netting; does not prescribe if no pain complaint.
If the patient requests pain medication, the agent first completes safety checks, then recommends acetaminophen dosing guidance and explicitly avoids NSAIDs; no other prescriptions made.
steps: EDUCATE (diagnosis + lab caveat + emergency plan + NSAID avoidance + lifestyle)
